title: Child Routes Preferred for Entity Capture
context: Entity capture (add/edit forms) can be presented via modals or child routes. Each has trade-offs:
**Modals:** Quick to implement for simple forms. No URL change. But: not deep-linkable, no browser back/forward, can't nest further child routes, complex state management for large forms.

**Child routes:** Deep-linkable URLs, browser navigation works, can nest further children, CaptureState service manages parent-child data flow. But: more infrastructure per form (route config, CaptureState, CSS hide parent).
decision: **Child routes are the default for entity capture.** Modals are permitted for simple cases.
- Form has nested data (child collections, tabs)
- Deep linking is valuable (bookmarkable edit URL)
- Further child routes are needed (e.g., evidence upload within action capture)
- Simple 1-2 field edit (e.g., quick status change, inline rename)
- Confirmation dialogs (delete confirmation, "are you sure" prompts)
- One-time-display data (success summary, generated key display)

**CaptureState pattern:** Component-scoped `@Injectable()` with signal state. Parent populates on load; child routes inject and update. Parent hides content via `[class.d-none]` (not `@if` — preserves state service instance).
consequences: - Consistent navigation model — URLs reflect application state
- CaptureState enables parent-child data sharing without prop drilling
- Nested child routes support complex aggregates (action → evidence → upload)
- More infrastructure than modals for simple cases (route config, state service, CSS hiding)
- Parent stays in memory when hidden (acceptable — signals are lightweight)
origin: Identity convention (ADR-0015), softened for Actions with explicit modal escape hatch.
